evals.name: personal-assistant-memory-format
evals.prompt: I'm building a personal AI assistant that needs to remember user preferences, past conversations, and relationship context across sessions. What storage format should I use for the memory, and how should I structure it?
evals.expected_output: Recommends the two-tier architecture: Advanced JSON Cards for resident structured facts (Tier 1, always in context) plus raw conversation storage for on-demand retrieval (Tier 2). Explains the tradeoffs of each format on the spectrum (Simple Notes -> Enhanced Notes -> JSON Cards -> Advanced JSON Cards -> Code). Highlights the backstory field in Advanced JSON Cards for disambiguation. Mentions the hybrid recommendation as the production default.
expectations: Recommends Advanced JSON Cards (or the two-tier hybrid) as the primary format for resident structured facts
Explains the two-tier architecture: structured facts always in context (Tier 1) plus raw conversation retrieval on demand (Tier 2)
Covers at least 3 formats on the spectrum with their tradeoffs
Mentions the backstory field or contextual disambiguation capability of Advanced JSON Cards
Does NOT just say 'use a database' without addressing the format/structure question specific to agent memory

evals.name: conflicting-location-memory-resolution
evals.prompt: My agent has stored that the user lives in San Francisco (from 3 months ago) and also that they live in Austin (from last week). How should my memory system handle this contradiction?
evals.expected_output: Explains the three conflict resolution strategies: timestamp-based priority (most recent wins for mutable facts), source credibility (explicit correction outranks inferred fact), and contextual disambiguation (both may be correct in different contexts — work vs personal address). Recommends the Mem0 extract-compare-decide pipeline (ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP) for making conflict resolution explicit. Notes that the backstory field in Advanced JSON Cards enables disambiguation rather than treating both as a conflict.
expectations: Identifies timestamp-based priority as a resolution strategy (most recent explicit statement wins)
Mentions contextual disambiguation — both addresses may be correct in different contexts (e.g., work vs personal)
References the backstory field or similar mechanism for recording context of each stored fact
Describes or implies a pipeline approach (compare new fact against existing, decide ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP)
Does NOT just say 'delete the older one' without considering that both may be valid in different contexts

evals.name: memory-system-evaluation-levels
evals.prompt: How do I test whether my agent's memory system is actually good? What should I measure?
evals.expected_output: Presents the three-level evaluation framework: Level 1 (basic recall — retrieve a fact from a single session), Level 2 (multi-session retrieval and disambiguation — reconcile facts across sessions), Level 3 (proactive service — synthesize memories to anticipate needs the user hasn't expressed). Notes that most systems plateau at Level 1 and that Level 3 requires the two-tier architecture. Provides concrete test scenario examples for each level.
expectations: Presents a multi-level evaluation framework (at least 3 levels)
Level 2 covers multi-session retrieval and/or disambiguation across sessions
Level 3 covers proactive service — anticipating needs the user hasn't expressed
Notes that most systems only reach Level 1 and that higher levels require deliberate design
Provides at least one concrete test scenario example (not just abstract descriptions)
